brief: modern mercantilism, AI, or their intersection (the Bridgewater 'Forecasting the Future 2026' brief)

topics.title: The macro-financial loop: AI capex + industrial-policy spending → rates, debt, currency, inflation, and the monetary-statecraft endgame
topics.rationale: The required broad pillar and the single most Bridgewater-relevant node — where every AI and mercantilism thread cashes out into the variables an investor trades. Kept deliberately whole and broadened: the competing scenario endgames, the price-level mechanism, the institutional question of central-bank independence, the growth denominator, the bust transmission, and dollar/reserve fragmentation are all facets of one macro-financial system, not separate slots. Absorbs four macro lens-gaps (inflation regime, fiscal dominance, r-g sustainability, capex-bust) and the former standalone financial/currency-statecraft topic, because they are inseparable from the rates/debt/FX arithmetic they drive.
sub_questions: Which scenario endgame wins (orderly absorption vs fiscal crisis vs stagflation), and what does each imply for real rates and sovereign curves?
Inflation regime tug-of-war: does AI's disinflationary productivity shock offset the cost-push of tariffs, reshoring, and subsidy-fueled demand?
Fiscal dominance: do central banks retain autonomy to fight inflation, or do financing needs force financial repression / yield-curve management / politically pressured rates?
The r-g denominator: does an AI productivity boom lift trend growth enough to stabilize debt despite the spending wave?

Capex-bust transmission: would an AI investment correction (concentrated hyperscalers, vendor-financing circularity, private-credit funding) trigger a balance-sheet recession that swamps industrial-policy stimulus?
Dollar-system fragmentation and AI-enabled financial statecraft: reserve-currency erosion, sanctions enforcement, capital-flow surveillance, programmable-CBDC coercion.
candidate_forecasts: P(US 10y real yield exceeds a stress threshold for a sustained period by 2028)
P(a G7 central bank adopts explicit yield-curve control or overt financial-repression measure by 2028)
P(an AI-capex correction triggers a ≥20% broad-equity drawdown or a credit event by 2028)
P(USD share of allocated global FX reserves falls below 50% by 2030)

topics.title: Do AI scaling returns hold, and does the frontier model layer ossify into moats or commoditize?
topics.rationale: The foundational supply-side AI axis: whether continued compute investment keeps buying capability and who captures the resulting surplus. Every AI-sector and capex forecast is downstream. Retains the merged scaling-returns, model-layer-structure, and cost-per-capability cruxes. The fat-tail superlinear/decisive-advantage scenario is deliberately routed to the tail-discontinuity topic (T9) so this pillar can model the smooth hold-vs-commoditize continuum without being dominated by its own tail.
sub_questions: Do scaling returns to training compute continue to buy proportional capability through the forecast window?
Does the cost-per-capability curve keep falling, and how fast does inference cost collapse?
Does the frontier model layer entrench durable moats or commoditize toward parity among several labs?
Who captures the surplus — model labs, chipmakers, cloud, or application layer?
candidate_forecasts: P(frontier training compute keeps doubling on a <X-month cadence with commensurate capability gains through 2027)
P(≥3 firms sit within a capability-parity band of the leader by 2027)
P(inference cost per standardized task falls >10x by 2028)

topics.title: Agentic AI diffusion, labor-market disruption, the political-economy backlash, and talent as a contested resource
topics.rationale: The demand/society-side AI axis and a core macro signal: how fast autonomous agents diffuse, the productivity-vs-displacement balance, and how democracies respond — the feedback channel from AI into mercantilist trade and industrial policy. Broadened to fold up the high-skill-talent lens-gap, because the human-capital chokepoint (visa regimes, deemed-export rules, poaching, brain-drain/gain) is politically fused with the labor backlash: immigration restriction and labor protection are the same political-economy reaction, and talent is plausibly the highest-leverage input not otherwise on the board.
sub_questions: How fast do autonomous agents diffuse into production, and what is the productivity-vs-displacement balance?
How do democracies respond politically, and how does that backlash feed back into tariffs and industrial policy?
Talent chokepoint: do visa regimes, deemed-export rules, security walls, and active poaching function as explicit mercantilist tools?
Does the US sustain its talent magnet, or does immigration backlash colliding with labor disruption fragment it and shift the frontier?
candidate_forecasts: P(measurable economy-wide US productivity acceleration attributable to AI by 2028)
P(a major democracy enacts significant AI-driven labor protection or deployment restriction by 2028)
P(US net inflow of top-tier AI researchers changes direction relative to China by 2028)

topics.title: Is the post-1945 free-trade order permanently broken? Managed trade, bloc formation, and the fate of multilateral rules
topics.rationale: The foundational mercantilism axis — the bench's only pure home for the trade-architecture question that defines 'modern mercantilism.' Broad enough to host the WTO-survival, national-security-carve-out, and bloc-vs-bilateral cruxes as sub-questions. The AI-compute dimension of who-aligns-with-whom is split out into T8 so this pillar stays a clean single-axis trade-order anchor; historical instability of managed-trade blocs is folded in as a calibration sub-question.
sub_questions: Is the free-trade order permanently broken, or does it renegotiate back toward rules?
Does the WTO (esp. dispute settlement) survive as an effective institution?
Do national-security carve-outs become the default justification, swallowing the rules?
Bloc vs bilateral: do durable trade blocs form, and are they historically stable or prone to renegotiation?
candidate_forecasts: P(WTO dispute settlement remains effectively non-functional through 2028)
P(US effective tariff rate stays above a structural-break threshold through 2028)
P(a formal multilateral tech/trade bloc that excludes China forms by 2028)

topics.title: Does the Western tech-control paradigm hold? Compute export controls, open-weight diffusion, and sanctioned-state parallel systems
topics.rationale: The decisive mercantilism×AI crossing. Merges the former compute-chokepoint and open-weight topics: they are the two halves of one control-efficacy question — do controls bind, and does the weights-as-files channel make the whole compute-control paradigm misspecified? One deep explore can weigh control efficacy against every erosion channel at once (smuggling/transshipment, domestic substitution, open-weight proliferation, governance vacuums, non-state and Global-South diffusion). Absorbs the sanctioned-state lens-gap (Russia/Iran, China-anchored stack) as the leading indicator that tells you which way the erosion-vs-tightening question is resolving.
sub_questions: Do export controls constrain rivals, or erode faster than they tighten via smuggling, transshipment, and domestic substitution?
What survives when frontier-class models travel as open-weight files — governance vacuum, knowledge transfer, non-state and Global-South access?
Does weight diffusion render the compute-control paradigm fundamentally misspecified?
Sanctioned-state parallel-system building (Russia/Iran on Chinese chips + open weights + smuggling): viable workaround or stagnation, and does it anchor a second self-sufficient sphere?
candidate_forecasts: P(China manufactures a competitive sub-X-nm node at meaningful scale by 2028)
P(an open-weight model within X months of frontier capability is released by 2027)
P(the US net-tightens vs net-relaxes a major control category by 2027)
P(a sanctioned state demonstrates a domestically/Chinese-supplied frontier-adjacent compute stack by 2028)

topics.title: The AI buildout: industrial-policy subsidy races, the physical geography of fabs/datacenters/energy/capital — and the capacity to actually execute
topics.rationale: Consolidates where AI physically gets built and who funds it — subsidy races, semiconductor-manufacturing geography, datacenter/grid politics, minerals/energy nationalism, Gulf SWF capital, the hyperscaler-state nexus — into one infrastructure-and-capital area. Broadened with two material lens-gaps: state-capacity-to-execute (permitting, cost overruns, stranded fabs, captured subsidies, the historical failure rate of picking winners), which is the upstream determinant of whether the buildout and macro pillars even resolve as assumed; and quasi-sovereign firms (Nvidia/OpenAI/hyperscalers) negotiating sovereign-scale deals and conducting their own statecraft, which shifts the locus of mercantilist bargaining to firm-state hybrids and affects control adherence.
sub_questions: Who wins the subsidy races, and where do fabs, datacenters, energy, minerals, and capital physically concentrate?
Datacenter/grid politics and energy/minerals nationalism as binding constraints on the capability map.
State capacity: can governments actually deliver, or do cost overruns, permitting bottlenecks, regulatory capture, and stranded assets undermine the buildout?
Quasi-sovereign firms: how far do Nvidia/labs/hyperscalers negotiate state-to-state-scale deals semi-independently, and does that incentivize routing around export controls?
Military-AI demand as a driver of subsidy and export policy.
candidate_forecasts: P(a flagship subsidized fab misses output targets or is cancelled by 2028)
P(US datacenter power demand triggers a binding grid-driven siting constraint by 2027)
P(a frontier lab/chipmaker signs a sovereign-scale compute deal with a non-US/China state by 2027)

topics.title: Regulatory, standards, and data fragmentation as AI market protectionism — including the EU's fate, training-data legality, and protocol/interface capture
topics.rationale: The non-tariff-barrier lever crossed with AI: whether divergent regulation, data localization, and device/OS gatekeeping function as de facto protectionism that fragments the global AI market. Broadened to fold up three narrow but material mechanism-gaps into one 'rules as competitive weapon' area: the EU's trajectory (third regulatory pole vs squeezed rule-taker, and whether the Brussels effect survives when frontier capability sits outside Europe); training-data legality (fair-use vs TDM exceptions vs licensing walls) as a determinant of model cost and the geography of who can train; and offensive standard-setting / protocol-and-interface capture (agent rails, APIs, interop) that accrues SWIFT-style network rents and lock-in.
sub_questions: Do divergent regulation, data-localization, and device/OS gatekeeping fragment the AI market and entrench national champions?
EU's fate: genuine third pole with its own stack, or deindustrializing rule-taker as the Brussels-effect leverage erodes?
Training-data legality: do divergent copyright/TDM/licensing regimes become de facto industrial policy that shifts the model cost curve and the geography of who can train?
Protocol/interface capture: does one bloc's agent-protocol/API/interop stack become the default substrate for agentic commerce, embedding durable dependency?
candidate_forecasts: P(the EU is pushed into a net rule-taker position — consuming US/Chinese frontier models — by 2028)
P(a major jurisdiction's training-data ruling materially changes frontier model training cost by 2027)
P(one bloc's agent-protocol/interop stack becomes the de facto global default by 2028)

topics.title: Multipolar bloc formation: swing states, the Gulf compute pole, and digital non-alignment
topics.rationale: The largest actor gap and the corrective to a structurally US-China-bipolar bench. The actors who actually decide whether the world fractures into two blocs or many — the Gulf (UAE/G42, Saudi/Humain) deploying SWF capital + cheap energy to become a third compute pole, plus India, Indonesia, Brazil and other swing states practising digital non-alignment by buying from both sides. Whether they consolidate US-aligned, China-aligned, or genuinely hedged changes the bloc-formation forecast, the efficacy of export controls (Gulf as leakage/transshipment vector vs US-policed chokepoint), and where capex and demand physically land. Broad enough to be its own deep explore and distinct from the trade-order pillar (T4) and the buildout topic (T6).
sub_questions: Do swing states consolidate into US-aligned, China-aligned, or durably hedged poles?
Does the Gulf become a genuine third compute pole via SWF capital plus cheap energy?
Is the Gulf a control leakage/transshipment vector or a US-policed chokepoint?
How many poles does the system settle into, and where do capex and end-demand physically land?
candidate_forecasts: P(the Gulf hosts more than X GW of frontier-class compute by 2028)
P(a major swing state — e.g. India — formally tilts its AI stack to one bloc by 2028)
P(a US compute-access deal conditions Gulf buildout on excluding Chinese technology by 2027)

topics.title: Tail discontinuities and regime breaks: Taiwan/TSMC kinetic shock, decisive capability advantage, safety-catastrophe clampdown, and great-power war
topics.rationale: Every other topic forecasts a gradualist baseline; Bridgewater-style scenario analysis demands an explicit home for the regime breaks that would dominate, not merely adjust, the forecast — and the bench currently conditions its probability mass on continued peace and smooth capability growth without stating it. Bundles four high-impact tail lens-gaps that share a structure (discrete shock collapses the multipolar/managed-trade framework) into one broad area: a kinetic shock to Taiwan/TSMC that removes leading-edge silicon for all blocs at once; a discontinuous capability jump / decisive strategic advantage handing one bloc a runaway lead; a realized AI-safety/misuse catastrophe triggering an emergency compute clampdown or moratorium; and great-power war / the military-economic nexus. Also incorporates the 'competitive racing erodes the safety commons' mechanism as the pathway into the catastrophe branch.
sub_questions: Kinetic shock to Taiwan/TSMC (blockade, invasion, seizure, or denial): probability and the simultaneous compute-supply cliff for all blocs.
Discontinuous capability jump / decisive strategic advantage: does a recursive/research-automation feedback loop hand one actor a durable lead, collapsing multipolar balancing?

Does a discrete AI-safety/misuse catastrophe flip the regime from race-to-deploy to freeze-and-license (compute caps, KYC-on-FLOPs, IAEA-style authority), and is the clampdown cooperative or weaponized?
Does mercantilist racing systematically erode the safety commons, raising the odds of the catastrophe branch?
Great-power war and the military-economic nexus as a driver and as a discontinuity.
candidate_forecasts: P(a Taiwan blockade or kinetic event disrupts TSMC advanced-node output by 2030)
P(a discrete AI incident triggers a national or multilateral compute moratorium / licensing regime by 2028)
P(one bloc achieves a widely-acknowledged decisive capability lead by 2030)
